name: Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
subject: Chemistry

sections.heading: Carbonyl Group — Structure and Nomenclature
sections.body:
Aldehydes (R–CHO) and ketones (R–CO–R') share the carbonyl group >C=O. The C is sp² hybridised; one σ and one π bond between C and O. Polar bond (δ+ on C, δ− on O) → reactive towards nucleophiles.

Carboxylic acid (R–COOH): contains –C(=O)–OH; the OH is much more acidic than alcohol due to resonance stabilisation of carboxylate.

IUPAC names:

  • Aldehydes: replace -e of alkane with -al (no locant: CHO is always C1). Example: CH3CHO = ethanal; C6H5CHO = benzaldehyde (retained).
  • Ketones: -one suffix, with locant for C=O. CH3COCH3 = propan-2-one (acetone). Aryl: 1-phenylethan-1-one (acetophenone).
  • Carboxylic acids: -oic acid suffix (–COOH always C1). HCOOH = methanoic, CH3COOH = ethanoic, C6H5COOH = benzoic.

sections.heading: Preparation of Aldehydes and Ketones
sections.body:
Common methods:

  • Oxidation of 1° alcohol → aldehyde (PCC, or controlled K2Cr2O7); 2° alcohol → ketone.
  • Dehydrogenation of alcohols over Cu/573 K.
  • Hydration of alkynes (HgSO4, dil. H2SO4): ethyne → acetaldehyde; higher alkyne → ketone (Markovnikov).
  • Ozonolysis of alkenes: reductive workup (Zn/H2O) gives aldehydes/ketones.

Aldehydes from acyl chlorides — Rosenmund reduction: RCOCl + H2/Pd-BaSO4 (poisoned with S/quinoline) → RCHO. Catalyst poison stops over-reduction to alcohol.

Stephen reaction: RCN + SnCl2/HCl → RCH=NH·HCl → RCHO + NH3 (on hydrolysis).

Aldehydes/ketones from nitriles via Grignard: R'MgX + R–CN → R–C(=N–MgX)–R' → ketone (after H3O⁺).

Aromatic aldehydes:

  • Etard reaction: toluene + CrO2Cl2 in CS2 → benzaldehyde (after hydrolysis). Mild, no over-oxidation.
  • Gattermann–Koch: benzene + CO + HCl in presence of AlCl3/CuCl → benzaldehyde.

sections.heading: Physical Properties and Reactivity
sections.body:
Aldehydes/ketones are polar (μ ≈ 2.7 D) but cannot H-bond among themselves — boiling points lie between alkanes and alcohols of similar size. Lower members are water-soluble (H-bond acceptors). Carboxylic acids form cyclic dimers via two H-bonds in vapour phase, giving abnormally high b.p. Lower acids (HCOOH, CH3COOH) miscible with water.

The carbonyl carbon is electrophilic; nucleophiles attack at C, while H+ (or other electrophiles) bind O. Aldehydes are more reactive than ketones towards nucleophilic addition because: (i) alkyl groups in ketones donate electrons into C, lowering δ+; (ii) two alkyl groups cause more steric hindrance.

sections.heading: Nucleophilic Addition Reactions
sections.body:
General: Nu⁻ + >C=O → Nu–C–O⁻ → (after H⁺) Nu–C–OH.

  • HCN → cyanohydrin (NaCN/H+; useful synthon → α-hydroxy acids).
  • NaHSO3 → bisulphite addition product (white crystalline; used for purification of aldehydes and methyl ketones).
  • Alcohols (acid catalysis): RCHO + R'OH → hemiacetal → acetal RCH(OR')2. Ketones similarly give ketals.
  • Grignard reagents: → 1°/2°/3° alcohols (already discussed in Ch 7).
  • Reduction: H2/Ni, NaBH4, LiAlH4 reduce >C=O to >CH–OH. Clemmensen (Zn-Hg/HCl) and Wolff–Kishner (NH2NH2/KOH-glycol) reduce >C=O directly to >CH2.

Reactions with NH3 derivatives: H2N–Z + >C=O → >C=N–Z + H2O. With NH2OH → oxime; NH2NH2 → hydrazone; 2,4-DNP → 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazone (yellow/orange — test for >C=O); semicarbazide → semicarbazone.

sections.heading: Reactions Due to α-Hydrogen and Other Important Reactions
sections.body:
The α-H of an aldehyde/ketone is acidic (carbonyl stabilises the resulting enolate by resonance).

Aldol condensation: aldehydes/ketones with at least one α-H, in presence of dilute base (NaOH), give β-hydroxy carbonyl (aldol). On heating, water is eliminated to give an α,β-unsaturated carbonyl. Example: 2 CH3CHO → CH3CH(OH)CH2CHO → CH3CH=CHCHO (crotonaldehyde).

Cross aldol: between two different carbonyls; gives mixture unless one of them has no α-H (e.g. PhCHO + acetaldehyde → cinnamaldehyde mainly).

Cannizzaro reaction: aldehydes without α-H (HCHO, PhCHO) undergo disproportionation in concentrated NaOH: 2 PhCHO + NaOH → PhCH2OH + PhCOONa.

Oxidation:

  • Aldehydes are easily oxidised to acids by mild oxidants. Tollen's test: aldehyde + [Ag(NH3)2]⁺ → silver mirror + RCOO⁻. Fehling's test: aldehyde + Cu²⁺ tartrate complex → Cu2O brick-red ppt + RCOO⁻ (works for aliphatic aldehydes; aromatic give negative).
  • Ketones resist mild oxidation; strong oxidants cleave C–C bonds.
  • Iodoform test: methyl ketones (CH3CO–R) and ethanol/CH3CHOH–R give yellow CHI3 on warming with I2/NaOH. Useful diagnostic for –COCH3 or –CH(OH)CH3.

Electrophilic substitution on benzaldehyde: –CHO is deactivating and meta directing.

sections.heading: Carboxylic Acids — Preparation
sections.body:
• Oxidation of 1° alcohols and aldehydes (KMnO4, K2Cr2O7).
  • Oxidation of alkylbenzenes (KMnO4) → benzoic acid (regardless of side-chain length, provided benzylic H present).
  • Hydrolysis of nitriles: R–CN + H2O/H+ or OH⁻ → R–COOH.
  • Hydrolysis of esters, amides, acid chlorides, anhydrides.
  • Carboxylation of Grignard: R–MgX + CO2 → RCOO⁻MgX⁺ →H+ RCOOH.

sections.heading: Acidity and Reactions of Carboxylic Acids
sections.body:
Carboxylic acids are weak acids (pKa 4–5) but stronger than phenols and alcohols because the carboxylate ion is stabilised by equivalent resonance structures (negative charge spread over two equivalent oxygens).

Substituent effects: electron-withdrawing groups (–Cl, –NO2, –CN) increase acidity; electron-donating groups (–CH3, –OCH3) decrease it. Examples: CH3COOH (4.76) > HCOOH (3.75)? — actually HCOOH is stronger (no +I from R). Cl3CCOOH (0.66) ≫ CH3COOH. For substituted benzoic acids, –NO2 at o/p increases acidity strongly; –OCH3 at p decreases it.

Reactions:

  • With NaHCO3 → CO2 effervescence (distinguishes from phenols, which need NaOH).
  • Esterification (Fischer): RCOOH + R'OH ⇌ RCOOR' + H2O (conc. H2SO4).
  • Conversion to acid chloride: RCOOH + SOCl2 → RCOCl. Also PCl5, PCl3.
  • Conversion to anhydride: 2 RCOOH →P2O5 (RCO)2O.
  • Conversion to amide: via NH4⁺ salt → RCONH2 (heat).
  • Reduction with LiAlH4 (or B2H6) → 1° alcohol; with HI/red P → alkane (via CH3).
  • Decarboxylation: RCOO⁻Na⁺ + soda lime (NaOH/CaO) → RH + Na2CO3.
  • Hell–Volhard–Zelinsky reaction: RCH2COOH + Cl2/red P → RCHCl–COOH (α-halogenation).
  • Ring substitution on benzoic acid: –COOH is deactivating and meta-directing.

summary: Aldehydes (R–CHO) and ketones (R–CO–R') share the polar carbonyl group; carboxylic acids (R–COOH) extend this with an –OH that is acidic. Aldehydes are made by controlled oxidation (PCC), Rosenmund (RCOCl + H_2/Pd-BaSO_4), Stephen (RCN + SnCl_2/HCl), Etard, and Gattermann–Koch. Ketones from 2° alcohol oxidation, alkyne hydration, Grignard with nitriles. The carbonyl C is electrophilic — nucleophilic addition with HCN, NaHSO_3, alcohols (acetal), Grignard, hydride reductants, ammonia derivatives (oxime, hydrazone, 2,4-DNP, semicarbazone). α-H acidity drives aldol condensation; aldehydes lacking α-H undergo Cannizzaro disproportionation. Tollen's, Fehling's, and iodoform tests are diagnostic. Carboxylic acids are stronger acids than alcohols/phenols because of resonance-stabilised carboxylate; HVZ α-halogenation, esterification, conversion to RCOCl/RCONH_2, LiAlH_4 reduction, decarboxylation are key reactions.
pitfalls: Forgetting that ketones don't give Tollen's, Fehling's, or Schiff's tests — these are aldehyde-specific.
Iodoform test: works only for CH_3CO–R or CH_3CH(OH)–R (and ethanol/acetaldehyde) — not for all carbonyls.
Naming aldehyde with a locant — CHO is always C1.
Predicting that LiAlH_4 reduces –COOH to –CHO — it goes all the way to –CH_2OH; use DIBAL-H to stop at aldehyde.
Using NaOH on phenol vs NaHCO_3 on carboxylic acid — only acid liberates CO_2 from bicarbonate.
Getting Cannizzaro mixed up: needs concentrated NaOH and an aldehyde without α-H (HCHO, PhCHO).
Forgetting Saytzeff/Markovnikov when generating aldehyde vs ketone via alkyne hydration.
